data_flow_trace.original: Governance executes a proposal calling StakingConfig.setMinimumStake(huge_value) at src/runtime/StakingConfig.sol:89
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: There is NO setMinimumStake(uint256) function in StakingConfig.sol. The contract exposes only initialize() (GENESIS-only, one-shot), setForNextEpoch(...) (GOVERNANCE, stages PendingConfig), and applyPendingConfig() (RECONFIGURATION-only, applies at epoch boundary).
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Callable surface for GOVERNANCE is limited to setForNextEpoch at line 130-147; this function never writes minimumStake directly — it writes _pendingConfig and sets hasPendingConfig = true.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Immediate setter removed; replaced by pending-config pattern that mirrors the other 7 config contracts the issue author cites.

data_flow_trace.original: minimumStake is written immediately to storage at src/runtime/StakingConfig.sol:95 — no pending stage, no epoch boundary wait
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Line 95 'minimumStake = _minimumStake' exists ONLY inside initialize() (GENESIS one-shot, gated by `if (_initialized) revert AlreadyInitialized()` at line 92). Post-genesis updates flow through setForNextEpoch (line 139-146: writes _pendingConfig, not minimumStake) and only applyPendingConfig (line 161, guarded by requireAllowed(RECONFIGURATION) at line 157) commits the change to the live minimumStake slot.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.GENESIS) at line 91 + _initialized guard at line 92 for immediate write; requireAllowed(RECONFIGURATION) at line 157 for applyPendingConfig.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: The 'immediate storage write' path is no longer reachable by GOVERNANCE.

data_flow_trace.original: A user calls Staking.createStakePool() at src/staking/Staking.sol:183 in the same epoch
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: createStakePool still exists and still reads IStakingConfig.minimumStake() at line 195. But since minimumStake cannot be changed mid-epoch by GOVERNANCE (it is only updatable via applyPendingConfig at epoch boundary), the read returns the pre-existing epoch value.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: No mid-epoch mutation possible upstream; createStakePool is itself blocked during transition by `isTransitionInProgress()` guard at line 186-188.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Behavior unchanged at this layer, but the upstream precondition (mid-epoch minimumStake mutation) is no longer achievable.

data_flow_trace.original: Staking reads IStakingConfig.minimumStake() at src/staking/Staking.sol:185, gets the new huge value
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: The read returns the currently-committed minimumStake; since GOVERNANCE cannot commit a new value mid-epoch (only stage it via setForNextEpoch), no 'new huge value' can appear until RECONFIGURATION invokes applyPendingConfig at the epoch boundary.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Value changes only via applyPendingConfig (line 156-171), which is RECONFIGURATION-gated.

data_flow_trace.original: The user's msg.value < new minimumStake, so the call reverts at src/staking/Staking.sol:186-187 with InsufficientStakeForPoolCreation
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: The revert branch exists (line 196-198) but only triggers against the stable, epoch-boundary-committed minimumStake, not against an attacker-triggered mid-epoch spike.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Revert no longer constitutes a 'mid-epoch DoS' since the threshold cannot be moved mid-epoch.

data_flow_trace.original: Unlike all other 7 config contracts, StakingConfig has no pending pattern — the TODO at line 13 acknowledges this gap
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: The alleged TODO is gone. The NatSpec at line 10-12 now explicitly states: 'Uses pending config pattern: changes are queued and applied at epoch boundaries.' A PendingConfig struct (line 29-35), _pendingConfig storage (line 59), hasPendingConfig flag (line 62), setForNextEpoch (line 130-147), and applyPendingConfig (line 156-171) all implement the pending pattern. StakingConfig is no longer the 'sole exception'.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Core premise of the finding is inverted by the current code.

reasoning_chain: The vulnerability is fully fixed. Step 1 is broken because no setMinimumStake(uint256) function exists in StakingConfig.sol — the only GOVERNANCE-callable mutator is setForNextEpoch (src/runtime/StakingConfig.sol:130-147), which writes the staged PendingConfig struct at lines 139-146 and sets hasPendingConfig = true, but never touches the live minimumStake slot. Step 2 is broken by two guards: the immediate-write path at line 95 is inside initialize() and gated by requireAllowed(GENESIS) (line 91) plus `if (_initialized) revert AlreadyInitialized()` (line 92); the live-slot update at line 161 is inside applyPendingConfig, gated by requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.RECONFIGURATION) (line 157), so GOVERNANCE cannot cause a mid-epoch write. Steps 4-5 collapse because without a mid-epoch mutation, the createStakePool read at src/staking/Staking.sol:195 returns the stable epoch value. Step 6's core premise — that StakingConfig lacks the pending pattern — is explicitly falsified by the NatSpec 'Uses pending config pattern' at line 12 and the presence of PendingConfig/hasPendingConfig/setForNextEpoch/applyPendingConfig (lines 29-35, 62, 130-147, 156-171).

key_evidence.excerpt: function setForNextEpoch(uint256 _minimumStake, uint64 _lockupDurationMicros, uint64 _unbondingDelayMicros) external { requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.GOVERNANCE); _requireInitialized(); _validateConfig(...); _pendingConfig = PendingConfig({ minimumStake: _minimumStake, ... }); hasPendingConfig = true; emit PendingStakingConfigSet(); }
key_evidence.why_matters: This is the only GOVERNANCE-callable mutator. It stages into _pendingConfig, never writes live minimumStake — exactly the pending pattern the issue claims is missing.

key_evidence.excerpt: function applyPendingConfig() external { requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.RECONFIGURATION); _requireInitialized(); if (!hasPendingConfig) return; minimumStake = _pendingConfig.minimumStake; ... hasPendingConfig = false; delete _pendingConfig; emit StakingConfigUpdated(); emit PendingStakingConfigCleared(); }
key_evidence.why_matters: Only path that writes the live minimumStake post-genesis; RECONFIGURATION-gated, invoked at epoch boundary — matches the invariant the issue author claims is broken.

key_evidence.excerpt:
/// @dev Initialized at genesis, updatable via governance (GOVERNANCE).
///      Uses pending config pattern: changes are queued and applied at epoch boundaries.
key_evidence.why_matters: Documents the very pattern the issue claims is absent; directly contradicts the finding's premise.

key_evidence.excerpt: if (IReconfiguration(SystemAddresses.RECONFIGURATION).isTransitionInProgress()) { revert Errors.ReconfigurationInProgress(); } ... uint256 minStake = IStakingConfig(SystemAddresses.STAKE_CONFIG).minimumStake(); if (msg.value < minStake) { revert Errors.InsufficientStakeForPoolCreation(msg.value, minStake); }
key_evidence.why_matters: Downstream read is unchanged, but it observes only the epoch-boundary-committed value; the transition-in-progress guard further ensures no observation during apply.

counterargument: A skeptic could argue: even with the pending pattern, governance can still stage a huge minimumStake via setForNextEpoch, and once RECONFIGURATION calls applyPendingConfig at the next epoch boundary the value becomes effective, potentially DoS-ing createStakePool thereafter. However, this is no longer the 'mid-epoch, in-flight' scenario the issue describes — it is a deliberate governance policy choice taking effect atomically at an epoch boundary, which is exactly the invariant the issue claims was violated. Additionally, setForNextEpoch calls _validateConfig (line 137) which enforces _minimumStake != 0 but has NO upper bound, so an abusive 'huge value' proposal is still possible post-epoch; but that is governance trust, not a mid-epoch bypass. Finally, a tiny residual concern: setForNextEpoch overwrites any previously staged PendingConfig without a cooldown, so governance could keep re-staging values — still bounded by epoch boundary semantics and thus out of scope for this specific finding.
